1: YHWH’s first challenge: “shall a faultfinder contend with the Almighty?” — the direct question that puts Job’s legal claim to the test. Romans 9:20: “who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, ‘why have you made me like this?’” The challenge to the faultfinder is answered in the NT not by human silence alone but by Christ’s perfect advocacy: only the one who himself made no fault (1 Peter 2:22) could properly plead before God. Job’s silence (v4–5) anticipates the silence of all accusation before Christ’s perfect righteousness.
2: He who argues with God, let him answer — the demand for a real legal response. In the NT, the one who argues with God from a position of genuine righteousness is Christ himself: Hebrews 5:7 (“in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death”). Christ argues with God not from self-justification but from perfect prayer and submission; his cry of dereliction (Matthew 27:46) is the deepest legal argument ever made before God, and the resurrection is God’s answer.
3: Job answers YHWH and says — the moment of the creature’s response before the Creator. This structural moment (creature speaking to Creator after the divine speeches) has its NT parallel in every prayer: the creature who has heard the Creator’s self-revelation in Christ now speaks in response. Ephesians 2:18: “through him [Christ] we both have access in one Spirit to the Father.” Job’s answer is the model of creaturely response: from within the overwhelming encounter, with diminished argument but with genuine address.
4: “Behold, I am of small account; what shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth” — the silencing of the creature before the Creator. Philippians 2:10–11: “at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.” The silenced mouth that lays its hand before God in Job 40:4 is the prototype of the universal confession before Christ at the parousia; the creature’s recognition of its smallness before the divine reaches its fullest expression in the acknowledgment of Christ’s lordship.
5: Once I have spoken and will not answer, twice and will add nothing more — the economy of the humbled creature’s speech. Matthew 6:7: “when you pray, do not heap up empty phrases as the Gentiles do.” Luke 18:13: the tax collector who would not even lift his eyes but simply said “God, be merciful to me, a sinner.” Job’s silenced brevity is the model of genuine prayer: not the accumulation of legal arguments but the honest acknowledgment of creatureliness before God, which is itself an approach to the prayer that Christ teaches.
6: YHWH answering Job from the whirlwind a second time — the second divine speech. The double divine address from the storm is the structure of divine revelation: the first address is the question that humbles; the second address is the deeper revelation that heals. In the NT, the double speaking of the Father at Jesus’ baptism (Matthew 3:17) and transfiguration (Matthew 17:5) follows the same pattern of progressive divine revelation that discloses the Son more fully in the second address.
7: Gird up your loins like a man; I will question you and you make it known — the renewed call to courageous encounter. 1 Peter 1:13: “preparing your minds for action (lit. girding up the loins of your mind), being sober-minded, set your hope fully on the grace that will be brought to you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.” The loins-girding that YHWH demands of Job in the storm becomes, in the NT, the mental preparation for the parousia. The courageous encounter with God is now undertaken through hope in Christ’s revelation.
8: “Will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be in the right?” — the sharpest question of the divine speeches. Romans 3:4: “let God be true though every one were a liar, as it is written, ‘that you may be justified in your words, and prevail when you are judged.’” Paul quotes Psalms 51:4 here; the question that YHWH poses to Job — will you condemn God to justify yourself? — is answered by the gospel in the opposite direction: God justifies the sinner at the cost of condemning his own Son (Romans 8:3).
9: Do you have an arm like God’s, can you thunder with a voice like his? — the question of divine power. Revelation 1:15: “his voice was like the roar of many waters.” The thundering divine voice of the whirlwind is the voice of the risen Christ in Revelation. The divine power (arm, voice, thunder) that only God possesses is the power that Christ shares with the Father (John 10:30: “I and the Father are one”; John 5:19: “whatever the Father does, the Son does likewise”).
10: Adorn yourself with majesty and dignity, clothe yourself with glory and splendor — the call to put on divine royal attributes. Philippians 2:9–11: God has highly exalted Christ and given him the name above every name. The divine majesty, dignity, glory, and splendor that Job cannot clothe himself in are the very attributes that Christ shares from eternity and puts back on at his exaltation after the cross. 1 Timothy 6:16: “who alone has immortality, who dwells in unapproachable light.”
11: Pour out the overflowings of your anger; look on everyone who is proud and abase him — the divine prerogative to judge the proud. James 4:6: “God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.” Luke 1:52: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate.” The divine prerogative to abase the proud that Job cannot exercise is the prerogative that Christ’s cross and parousia exercise definitively: the cross humbles the proud by revealing that no human achievement merits justification; the parousia abases every earthly pride before the returning King.
12: Look on everyone who is proud and bring him low; tread down the wicked where they stand — the divine judgment that Job cannot execute. John 5:22: “the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son.” Acts 17:31: God “has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed.” The judgment of the proud and wicked that YHWH challenges Job to execute is the judgment that the Father has given to Christ: the Son will tread down the wicked at the appointed day.
13: Hide them all in the dust together; bind their faces in the world below — the disposal of the condemned. Revelation 20:14–15: “then Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death.” The dust-binding and Sheol-binding that YHWH describes as his judicial prerogative is the prerogative that Christ exercises at the final judgment: the disposal of all that opposes the kingdom is the divine act committed to the Son (1 Corinthians 15:25: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet”).
14: “Then will I also praise you, for your own right hand can save you” — the ironic conditional: if Job can execute universal judgment, then God will acknowledge Job’s self-sufficiency. The right hand that saves in the OT is YHWH’s (Psalms 98:1: “his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation for him”); in the NT, the right hand is Christ’s position (Hebrews 1:3: “he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high”) after accomplishing the salvation that no human right hand could achieve.
15: “Look at Behemoth, which I made as I made you” — the creature solidarity of Behemoth and Job, both made by the same Creator. Philippians 2:7: “taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men.” The “made as I made you” — creaturely solidarity between the powerful and the vulnerable — is the OT type of the Incarnation: the eternal Son took on the same creaturely status (“likeness of men”) as those he came to redeem. The Creator becoming a creature is the supreme expression of the solidarity that v15 implies.
16: His strength is in his loins, his power in the muscles of his belly — Behemoth’s physical power concentrated in the body’s core. Ephesians 6:10: “be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might.” The strength of Behemoth that no human warrior can match is the strength that Christ makes available to those in him — not through physical might but through the Spirit: “strengthened with power through his Spirit in your inner being” (Ephesians 3:16). The interior power that Behemoth displays externally, Christ supplies inwardly to the believer.
17: His tail moves like a cedar, the sinews of his thighs are knit together — the extraordinary strength of Behemoth’s body structure. 1 Corinthians 15:44: “it is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body.” The resurrection body that Christ was first to receive (v20: “the firstfruits”) is the perfection of creaturely embodiment: the glorified body that Behemoth’s extraordinary physicality faintly typifies. Christ’s resurrection body is the first example of the new creation’s embodied power (Philippians 3:21: “he will transform our lowly body to be like his glorious body”).
18: His bones are tubes of bronze, his limbs like bars of iron — the impenetrable body of Behemoth. Acts 2:24: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.” The bronze-bone, iron-limbed Behemoth that nothing can pierce is the creaturely type of Christ’s resurrection body that death could not hold. The “pangs of death” that could not contain Christ are the force that Behemoth’s impenetrable frame defies at a creaturely level.
19: “He is the first of the works of God; let him who made him bring near his sword” — Behemoth as the chief of the works of creation, challengeable only by his Maker. Colossians 1:15: “he is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.” Behemoth as “first of the works of God” (re’shit) is the creaturely type of Christ as “firstborn of all creation” (prototokos) — not the first creature made but the one who holds primacy over all creation. Only the Maker can bring the sword to Behemoth; only the cross could accomplish what creation could not.
20: The mountains yield their produce for him; all the wild beasts play where he lies — Behemoth as the center of the natural order. John 1:3: “all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.” The Behemoth that the natural world serves and gathers around is the creaturely type of Christ as the one through whom all things exist and toward whom all things ultimately move (Romans 11:36: “from him and through him and to him are all things”).
21: Under the lotus plants he lies, in the shelter of the reeds and in the marsh — Behemoth resting in the wetlands. John 1:14: “the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskenosen, tented/tabernacled) among us.” The great creature resting in the marsh’s shelter is the type of the Son taking his place in creaturely habitat. Christ who “tabernacled” among humanity rested in the specific ecological conditions of the Jordan valley and the reed-edged Sea of Galilee — the same terrain Behemoth inhabits.
22: The lotus trees cover him for shade; the willows of the brook surround him — the natural canopy over Behemoth. Psalms 91:4: “he will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge.” The shade-giving lotus over Behemoth is the type of the divine covering over those who take refuge in God. Matthew 23:37: the hen covering chicks under wings is Christ’s own image for his protection of Jerusalem. The Behemoth shaded in the marsh becomes, by inversion, the one who provides shade and protection for others.
23: If the river is turbulent he is not frightened; he is confident though Jordan rushes against his mouth — Behemoth’s confidence in the Jordan’s rushing flood. Matthew 3:13–17: Christ’s baptism in the Jordan River. The Jordan that Behemoth is not frightened by is the Jordan in which Christ was baptized — identifying with sinners under judgment and emerging to the Father’s declaration: “this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.” The confident creature in the Jordan typifies Christ’s confident submission to baptismal waters of judgment.
24: Can one take him by his eyes or pierce his nose with a snare? — the unhuntable Behemoth. Luke 11:22: the “stronger man” who comes upon the strong man and overcomes him takes his armor and divides the spoil. Behemoth cannot be caught; but the “stronger man” of Christ’s parable points to the one who can overcome the unhuntable — by going through death and resurrection rather than by conventional conquest. What no human snare can capture, the cross and resurrection accomplished.
